explanation.rule: The prefix un- is added to the beginning of adjectives and verbs to give the opposite or negative meaning.

groups.note: Added to adjectives to mean 'not'. It can also reverse the meaning of a verb (unlock, undo). Simply place un- before the base word with no spelling changes.

examples.definition: not happy; feeling sad or dissatisfied
not usual; different from what is normal
not fair; not treating people equally
not reliable; cannot be trusted or depended on

wordForms.definition: not happy; feeling sad or dissatisfied
wordForms.example: She was unhappy with the results of the exam.
wordForms.rootDefinition: feeling or showing pleasure or contentment

wordForms.definition: not able; lacking the skill or opportunity to do something
wordForms.example: He was unable to attend the meeting due to illness.
wordForms.rootDefinition: having the skill or opportunity to do something

wordForms.definition: not usual; different from what is normal or expected
wordForms.example: It is unusual to see snow here in April.
wordForms.rootDefinition: happening regularly or in the normal way

wordForms.definition: not expected; happening without warning or preparation
wordForms.example: The good news came as a completely unexpected surprise.
wordForms.rootDefinition: thought likely or planned to happen

wordForms.definition: not comfortable; causing physical or emotional discomfort
wordForms.example: The long flight was uncomfortable without a window seat.

wordForms.definition: not known; not familiar or identified
wordForms.example: The artist was unknown outside her home country.

wordForms.definition: not fair; not treating people equally or justly
wordForms.example: The students thought the exam marking was unfair.
wordForms.rootDefinition: treating people equally and according to the rules

wordForms.definition: not kind; somewhat cruel or inconsiderate
wordForms.example: It was unkind to make fun of his mistake in front of everyone.
Please avoid unnecessary delays in submitting the report.
wordForms.rootDefinition: needed or required for a purpose

wordForms.definition: not reliable; cannot be trusted to work properly or be on time
wordForms.example: The old bus service was unreliable and always late.
wordForms.rootDefinition: consistently good in quality; able to be trusted

multipleChoiceExercises.sentence: She was ___ with the quality of the meal and asked to speak to the manager.
Due to heavy traffic, he was ___ to reach the airport on time.
It is quite ___ to find a café open at six in the morning in this town.
The news of her promotion was completely ___ by her colleagues.
The hard plastic chair was ___ to sit on for three hours.
It was ___ to laugh at him when he made a simple mistake.

transformExercises.sentence: She looked ___ when she heard she had failed the test. (HAPPY)
The weather in March is always ___ — you never know what to expect. (USUAL)
The bus was so ___ that we decided to walk instead. (RELIABLE)
The new rule seemed ___ to many of the students in the class. (FAIR)
Winning the award was completely ___ — nobody had predicted it. (EXPECTED)
